sentiment.rationale: Habakkuk cries out about unanswered violence and injustice; God answers that He is raising up the Chaldeans as an instrument of judgment.
Five woes against the Chaldean oppressor, punctuated by the declaration that the just shall live by faith and the earth shall be filled with God's glory.
A majestic prayer recounting God's power at Sinai and in history, closing with triumphant faith despite total material loss.

missingCharacters.name: Habakkuk
missingCharacters.description: A Judahite prophet who wrestled with the problem of evil and received a vision of God's ultimate justice.

summaries.summary: Habakkuk cries to God about violence and injustice in Judah; God reveals He is raising up the Chaldeans as His instrument of judgment, provoking a second complaint.
God tells Habakkuk to write the vision: the just shall live by faith; five woes are pronounced upon the arrogant Chaldean oppressor.
Habakkuk's prayer-psalm recounts God's awesome power in nature and history, culminating in a declaration of rejoicing in the Lord despite complete loss.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes 'the just shall live by his faith' as the foundation of the gospel of grace.
Paul again cites this verse to prove that justification comes by faith, not by the law.
'The earth shall be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the Lord, as the waters cover the sea.'
'The Lord is in his holy temple: let all the earth keep silence before him.'

verses.reason: 'The just shall live by his faith' — quoted three times in the New Testament and central to Reformation theology.
'The earth shall be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the Lord, as the waters cover the sea.'
'The Lord is in his holy temple: let all the earth keep silence before him' — a widely used call to worship.
'Although the fig tree shall not blossom... yet I will rejoice in the Lord' — one of the Bible's most powerful declarations of faith in adversity.
